data.title: 5 Best REITs To Buy In October 2025
data.description:
The big news of this past month was that the Fed cut interest rates for the first time since December 2024.

Not only that, but they strongly implied in their commentary that more rate cuts would likely be needed in the near term. Here is what the Fed's Governor, Michelle Bowman, said:

"It's a lot easier to support the labor market by lowering the federal funds rate than it is to fix it after it's broken. It is time for the Committee to act decisively and proactively to address decreasing labor market dynamism and emerging signs of fragility." (emphasis added)

And they are right to be nervous about the job market. In September, private payrolls were expected to rise by 50,000, but they dropped by 32,000 instead. A huge miss.

As a result, the debt market is now predicting 5-6 additional rate cuts by the end of next year, bringing interest rates to the high 2s or low 3s:

This would be very good news for REITs (VNQ), particularly those that are heavily leveraged.

As a reminder, REITs have been in a bear market for 3+ years, causing their valuations to drop to historic lows, and this was almost entirely driven by the surge in interest rates of 2022 and 2023.

But if REITs crashed due to the rate hikes, this also means that they could now strongly recover as rates are cut, and those presenting the most upside potential are typically small-cap REITs with higher leverage and deeply discounted valuations.

Here are 5 such REITs to consider in October as we enter another cycle of monetary easing:

Armada Hoffler Properties (AHH)

Armada is a small-cap that focuses primarily on mixed-use properties, often combining retail, residential, and office under one roof. These are true class A trophy assets, typically located in walkable, high-barrier-to-entry locations of growing Sunbelt markets:

Its properties are performing well, enjoying high occupancy rates and steady rent growth. But what has heavily weighted down the company's valuation is its balance sheet, which is more leveraged than most other REITs, with a 7.4x Debt-to-EBITDA. This clearly concerns the market, and it explains the crash in the company's share price:

As a result, it now trades at just 6.5x FFO, an estimated 50% discount to its net asset value, and offers an 8% dividend yield that's well covered.

Such low valuations would only make sense if we remained in a higher interest rate environment and Armada turned into a value trap as it faced steadily rising interest expense.

But as interest rates are now cut down to materially lower levels, even as Armada also further deleverages its balance sheet, we expect a strong recovery over the coming years.

As it finishes its development projects, its debt-to-EBITDA is expected to drop to the mid-5s, which is far more reasonable, and this, coupled with 5-6 additional rate cuts, could easily push Armada back to 10-12x FFO.

This would result in 50-100% upside potential from here, and even at that level, it would still trade at a steep discount to most REITs. While you wait, you earn an 8% dividend yield.

Insiders clearly believe that the stock is undervalued, as they bought over $4 million worth of shares over the past year alone.

Whitestone REIT (WSR)

Whitestone owns mostly service-oriented neighborhood retail centers in rapidly growing Sunbelt markets like Phoenix, Austin, Dallas, and Houston:

Its properties enjoy sector-leading growth prospects, and the management has guided for steady 3-5% same-property NOI growth going forward.

This strong organic growth, coupled with some new accretive acquisitions, has the potential to result in 5-7% annual FFO per share growth going forward.

Even then, the market has heavily discounted Whitestone, primarily due to its balance sheet, which is still reasonable but more leveraged than most of its peers.

Its Debt-to-EBITDA is 7.1x, and this has concerned the market in recent years. The good news is that the company has made significant progress in deleveraging, with its Debt-to-EBITDA already dropping 3 turns, and they expect to reach 6.5x by the end of next year, which, coupled with significant rate cuts, should help their market sentiment.

We estimate their NAV at about $18 per share, and yet, you get to buy it today at just around $12, representing a 30% discount on desirable assets that are undersupplied and enjoying steady growth.

While you wait for the upside, you earn a 4.4% dividend yield, which, coupled with 5-7% FFO per share growth, has the potential to earn you double-digit total returns.

BSR REIT (HOM.U:CA / OTCPK:BSRTF)

BSR REIT is a very similar story. It is also a small-cap, primarily focused on Dallas, Houston, and Austin, but instead of buying neighborhood centers, it owns apartment communities:

Just like Whitestone, BSR has enjoyed sector-leading growth, and yet, it trades at a lower valuation than most of its peers, largely because of its more heavily leveraged balance sheet.

Its near 50% loan-to-value is still reasonable for such defensive assets, but it is higher than its peers.

However, the REIT is very prudent in that it retains a significant portion of its cash flow and has been selling assets, using some of those proceeds to deleverage and buy back shares to create value for shareholders.

Today, it is priced at an estimated 25% discount based on today's net asset value. But as interest rates are cut down further, even as its rent growth accelerates in 2026/2027, we expect its NAV to surge.

Based on its forward normalized 2026/2027 NAV, we think that the current discount is closer to 30-40%, and as conditions improve, we expect that gap to close, which could lead to up to 50% upside.

While you wait, you earn a 4.4% dividend yield, and the REIT keeps growing steadily.

Clipper Realty (CLPR)

Clipper has some similarities with BSR in that it is also a small REIT that focuses on affordable apartment communities that are performing well, but it is heavily discounted due to its balance sheet.

The difference is that Clipper focuses on NYC, and its balance sheet is even more heavily leveraged than that of BSR. On the flip side, it has enjoyed stronger rent growth in recent years due to the limited new supply in its markets, and its valuation is even cheaper than that of BSR.

Its Debt-to-EBITDA is sky-high at well over 10x, and its valuation is very low, trading at just 9x FFO and an estimated 50% discount to NAV.

As such, it is a highly leveraged play on interest rates at this point.

If rates are cut significantly over the next year, as the debt market is predicting, Clipper could see its share price surge 50-100% from here. But if rates remain higher for longer, Clipper could turn into a value trap and be forced to cut its dividend, currently yielding 10%.

It is a riskier play for investors with a higher risk tolerance, but we like the risk-to-reward, especially potentially going into a new easing cycle.

Macerich (MAC)

Macerich owns the highest quality malls in the US, even better than those of Simon Property Group (SPG) or Tanger (SKT).

They are located in more urban locations with higher barriers to entry and richer demographics. They also generate much higher sales per square foot:

Even then, it trades at a steep discount relative to its peers, and it is almost entirely due to its weaker balance sheet.

But the REIT is rapidly deleveraging its balance sheet by selling assets, handing back keys to lenders in select cases, retaining most of its cash flow, and growing its rental income.

By the end of 2028, they expect their Debt-to-EBITDA to drop to just 6.5x, which, coupled with significant rate cuts, could push the REIT to much higher levels.

We expect 2028 FFO per share to come in at right around $2 per share, which at a 15x multiple (in line with Tanger's current multiple) would push the share price to $30. That's nearly double today's price.

Closing Note

The Fed is back at cutting interest rates, and the cheapest REITs with the most leverage often offer the most upside potential.

They are set to win the most as interest rates are cut further, and we are positioning our portfolio accordingly at High Yield Landlord.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Mosaic's Pullback On Phosphate Production Struggles Provides An Appealing Entry Point
data.description:
We last looked at Mosaic (NYSE:MOS) in June, and I downgraded them from strong buy to buy on the basis of the stock having surged to near its 52-week high. In general though, I’ve been bullish about Mosaic’s fertilizer business, with expectations that between the expected recovery of Brazil’s economy and the generally high commodity prices, they’re well positioned to make good money, even if tariffs are a complicating factor.

Friday, we saw Mosaic drop a substantial amount on the release of preliminary Q3 volumes, which while an increase quarter over quarter were somewhat below guidance because of a mechanical issue at their Riverview plant in Florida. That’s going to be the subject of today’s analysis, and whether this reflects a substantial change in the investment thesis going forward or is simply a one-off problem that may provide a cheaper entry position than we’ve had in months.

Before we get into what we know about the Q3 production, though, I think it’s important to take a look at the earnings for the first two quarters of the year, because they’re better than you’d think.

Margins Improve on High Price

Mosaic was meant to scale up volume in the second half of the year, but earnings were already rather strong as a result of high commodity prices. We can see where the margins benefited substantially already in the first half.

1st Half 2024

1st Half 2025

Net Sales

$5.5 billion

$5.6 billion

Gross Profit

$793 million

$1.0 billion

Operating Income

$406 million

$583 million

Net Income

($116 million)

$649 million

GAAP EPS

(36¢)

$2.05

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

Though revenue wasn’t up much yet, we saw the margins benefit from higher prices in both potash and phosphates, with gross margins up from 14.4% to 17.9% and operating margins up from 7.4% to 10.4%.

On top of that Mosaic went from a $368 million charge for foreign currency loss in 2024 to a $302.5 million gain in 2025, which shifted them from treading water to raking in an enviable amount of income in the first half of the year.

The potash prices have remained strong through this period, and that’s a big part of the story of the first half of 2025. The second half is meant to be about Mosaic growing their phosphate production, and those prices remain strong as well, so the hope was for earnings to get even better.

Production Growth, But Not as Much as Hoped

It’s at this point we have to look at the guidance for phosphate production/sales in Q3. During the conference call for Q2 earnings, we were told to expect sales between 1.8 million and 2.0 million tonnes for the third quarter. What we got was somewhat less, though.

Citing the mechanical issues at Riverview and the utility outages at Bartow, Mosaic was only able to come in with production of around 1.7 million tonnes. On top of that, the amount shipped was only around 1.6 million tonnes, because of the lag between production and shipment at the end of the quarter.

On the one hand, this is an increase over the production we saw in Q2, and with phosphate prices roughly where they were last quarter. Given this, there is reason to expect that the earnings aren’t going to be calamitously bad. The problem is only that the guidance was higher still, and we didn’t reach it.

This suggests Mosaic is likely to come in a bit below estimates for Q3, at least on the phosphate side of things. We do see the company remaining strong on other fronts though, with potash coming up in-line with guidance at 2.3 million tonnes, and Mosaic Fertilizantes, the Brazil business, up 25% quarter-over-quarter to 2.8 million tonnes. With margins likely to remain on the strong side because prices remain high, I’m not overly pessimistic about Q3 estimates, even if we might come in marginally below the EPS estimate of $1.06 (non-GAAP). Friday’s sell-off seems like it more than prices in that eventuality.

The market reacted quickly, and RBC Capital Markets was quick to downgrade them on the news, lowering their price target to $30.00. They expressed concern that the operational issues may persist, though Mosaic in their own statement insisted that the problems were addressed and that they expect higher production rates going forward.

That is where known fact and worst-case scenario diverge. Though the mechanical problems are surely unwelcome, Mosaic is a huge producer with a long track record, and if they say the matter has been resolved, I think that’s something to consider more than just idle gossip.

RBC sees Mosaic as needing several strong quarters of production to win back investor confidence, but with the prices that we’re seeing on the phosphate and potash market, Mosaic has an opportunity to continue winning on the earnings side of things, and that, for me, covers a multitude of sins.

Given the shipped levels on Mosaic’s other products, and the report that the mechanical problems were resolved, I think the $30.00 price target is far too low, given the earnings Mosaic has enjoyed and should continue to enjoy, mechanical problems in Q3 notwithstanding.

What to Expect in Q3 Earnings

The next possible catalyst is the Q3 earnings, which are scheduled for November 4. Friday’s volumes portend a miss, though how substantial of one is the real question here. A roughly 10% sell-off on the news suggests they’re expecting a pretty big miss here, despite the potash and the Brazil numbers both coming in on the strong side.

If the earnings are more modest, and importantly if the next conference call reiterates that the production problems are behind them, that 10% may seem like too much, and Mosaic’s anticipated earnings could justify a recovery.

While investors may have hoped that Mosaic’s production would surge up sooner, rather than later, to take advantage of the high fertilizer prices, those prices have seemed resilient, and that means that even if we don’t see the full rate of production realized until Q4, the buyers are still there to pay Mosaic better-than-normal rates.

Bullish Case for Mosaic Intact, Just With a Lower Entry Price

Missing Q3 guidance might, in the end, be exactly the sort of entry level people who are bullish about Mosaic were looking for. The company has suggested this mechanical problem is behind them, and we have a chance to get back into the stock 10% cheaper than we did the day before.

All Mosaic has really lost, after all, is 200,000 to 300,000 tonnes of phosphate in Q3. If they’re correct that the production is back where it is meant to be, the earnings and, by extension, the share price should follow. I'm upgrading this back to a strong buy because, in my view, it's quite cheap at these levels.

Investors should keep a close eye on the margins in Q3, because those will tell the tale of how bad this production problem was. If they remain resilient, as they were in the first two quarters, it should be easier still to look beyond Q3 to their higher production levels in Q4 and beyond.

Far from a crisis, the Q3 volume miss might be the opportunity for a cheaper price that the longs were waiting for. Mosaic is trading well below their book value at this point, and their GAAP earnings should remain strong irrespective of the near-term weakness. For those willing to wait for a turnaround, this news and sell-off may look like a blip, and one not to be missed.

data.title: Ferrari: Capital Markets Day Was A Huge Disappointment (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
More than two hours into Ferrari's (NYSE:RACE) highly anticipated Capital Markets Day, a room full of analysts and a webcast with thousands of investors began to feel a little weird.

They've been hearing about Ferrari's uniqueness, history, heritage, ESG plans, and previous achievements all morning, but future financial targets have yet to be disclosed.

Perhaps it was a sign of what was about to come, as the targets that were eventually disclosed were a major letdown, sending shares into their worst selloff since 2016.

I argue investors should take a wait-and-see approach before jumping to capitalize on the selloff and downgrade the stock to 'Hold', as the Capital Markets Day reveals more structural issues than I previously thought.

Ferrari's Capital Markets Day Targets Shock Investors

Let's get right to it. From 2016 to 2024, Ferrari's revenues grew at a 10% CAGR, and from 2021 to 2024, the number is 16%. Meanwhile, net income grew at 18% and 22% CAGRs, respectively.

In its 2030 targets, Ferrari is telling investors that those days are over. For 2030, they are guiding revenues at €9 billion, EBITDA at €3.6 billion, EBIT at €2.75 billion, and EPS at €11.5. These targets reflect a very disappointing 5% CAGR for revenue through 2030, and a 5.2% CAGR for EPS.

In addition, the next big thing for Ferrari was supposed to be the fully electric Elettrica. In the 2022 Capital Markets Day, they said it is expected to be 40% of deliveries by 2030, a target that they've now cut in half.

Investors were clearly caught off guard, and shares took a 16% plunge yesterday, the worst selloff since Ferrari's IPO.

Ferrari Takes Conservatism To The Extreme, But Confidence Is Low After Q2

Long-time Ferrari investors should know that the company tends to be ridiculously conservative with its guidance. Look no further than their previous Capital Markets Day in 2022.

In that one, Ferrari guided for revenues of €6.7 billion, EBITDA of €2.6 billion, and EBIT of €1.9 billion. They achieved those targets already in 2024, two years in advance.

However, even then, they weren't as conservative. Their guidance reflected a 9% revenue CAGR, about a 600 bps improvement in margins, and almost a doubling in EPS.

Coming into Capital Markets Day, the bogey was revenue guidance at mid-to-high single digits, and Ferrari came far below.

As I wrote many times in previous articles, it has gotten to the point that analysts and investors almost completely ignore the company's guidance. However, following the recent disappointing second quarter, investors are less confident in underwriting large beats like they have been previously.

Management Commentary Didn't Help

Ahead of the Capital Markets Day, investors and analysts, knowing how Ferrari works, braced for prudent guidance. The questions were a) How prudent, and b) What will be the explanation.

We already answered the first question; let's get to the second one, using quotes from Ferrari's CFO.

To preserve exclusivity per model through our 4 product pillars, volume growth has been designed to remain very limited throughout the plan period.

One of the biggest concerns on investors' minds was Ferrari's intentions regarding volume growth. Well, there's your answer. Very limited volume growth means 0%-1%, at best. This makes Ferrari's path to consistent growth very challenging.

As usual, the real challenge is to design a product portfolio evolution for a smooth and linear development of our profitability despite the natural cadence of model launches and phaseout.

This is something I discussed in my last article. When you don't increase volumes and generally refrain from raising prices on the same model, you're left with the task of perfectly timing phase-ins and phase-outs of models that will deliver steady average-selling-price growth.

Notably, in 2025, the phase-ins and phase-outs are leading to minimal ASP growth, and with volumes being flat, that's how we got to the weak second quarter that caused a major selloff.

There could be a simple way to grow revenues and margins. For example, expanding the numbers of Icona and Special Series would be something that would help immediately. But then our business model would be destroyed very rapidly.

One clear thing is that Ferrari's management prioritizes scarcity above all else. They seem to be highly cautious of the possibility that they will dilute Ferrari's brand value in any way.

As outsiders, it's easy for us to be greedy and want them to release the valve, but I think investors should trust the management that delivered such exceptional results.

Still, the bottom line is that management didn't give investors much room to be overly confident about a sizeable beat. The one thing they repeatedly mentioned is that they assumed lower personalization penetration given the higher ASPs, and that they left room for agility in terms of technology investments.

Valuation & No Near-Term Catalysts

The main reason for my downgrade isn't the underwhelming guidance. I'm quite confident Ferrari will exceed its plan. I am downgrading the stock for two reasons.

Limited Upside

At the current stock price, Ferrari trades at 41 times forward earnings based on the 2025 guidance, and at an exit multiple of 31 based on the 2030 guide.

This is, without question, a lofty multiple for a company that's growing at a single-digit rate, let alone below 5%.

Historically, I justified Ferrari's multiple by its unparalleled brand value and consistent double-digit growth. Even if you're very bullish on Ferrari's exceeding its 2030 plan, I don't think it's reasonable to assume it will more than double its projections.

Lack Of Near-Term Catalysts

It will take some time for investors to get over the Capital Markets letdown. The best cure will be Ferrari delivering strong results, with accelerating revenue growth.

However, all arrows point to a third quarter that's even worse than Q2, as they continue to phase out the high-priced Daytona, and the F80 replacement is set to begin shipments only in Q4.

Meanwhile, they already upgraded their guidance for 2025, so there's not much they can do to drive enthusiasm, at least not until Q4.

Conclusion

Ferrari is still one of my favorite companies, and I still view it as arguably the widest-moat company in the world.

However, I'm no longer as bullish as I was on its ability to deliver market-beating financial results. It started showing cracks in Q2, and now it dropped the hammer with an extremely disappointing 2030 guidance.

Although I'm confident Ferrari will exceed its targets, the lack of near-term catalysts and a still demanding valuation give me pause.

As such, I downgrade Ferrari to 'Hold'.

data.title: Blend Labs: Slow Scaling Disappoints
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Blend Labs (NYSE:BLND) has a great opportunity to grow in the loan origination market. Industry heavyweights like ICE Mortgage Technology hold a large share, but Blend has been productive in taking market share.

The biggest obstacle now is scaling the revenues without adding to OPEX. This is doable, seeing as for every $5 million more in revenues, it adds about $3.65 million in gross profits. This could go straight to the bottom line if management can be conservative with OPEX spending and not issue any more debt. Since the company is yet to consistently post a profit, I'm hesitant to rate it a buy. I see the potential if it scales, but out of principle I won’t make it a buy. A hold seems more appropriate.

Stock Chart 3-Year Performance

BLND performed much worse than the S&P 500 and the Financial Select Sector ETF (XLF). But at times it outperformed somewhat, but has settled below the two other benchmarks. It has returned 28% over 3 years, a poor showing compared to 86% and 73%. It was on track to show similar returns, but the weak price action the past few weeks has shut this prospect down.

Short-term challenges exist as BLND still has a notable short interest: 5.65%, according to Seeking Alpha figures. The number spiked in August as the market reacted poorly to its last quarterly results, sending shares from $3.57 to $2.73. It bounced back a decent bit, but the bullish sentiment didn’t last long, and it’s now trading below pre-report levels. This supports a prolonged downtrend potentially starting for the stock, similar to what has occurred YTD.

I think it was a classic sell-the-news scenario because, looking at the numbers, BLND had a phenomenal quarter with 43% consumer banking growth. It was always a growth story, and perhaps the market also wanted to see even better numbers for the quarter. Or it was some of the cautious comments on Q3 results that spooked the market.

Product Analysis

BLND is a cloud-based platform that primarily services financial institutions and banks by improving the end-to-end journey. It acts as an all-in-one app that lets the consumer or lender do all of the necessary things relating to buying a house. All from having real estate agent communication in there to homeowner and title insurance.

Revenues are fee-based and generated on a per transaction basis, be that a loan origination or a bidding closure. Its consumer banking suite is the fastest-growing part of BLND right now, growing from $8 million in revenues in Q2 2024 to $11.4 million last quarter. It makes up about 30%, with the rest coming from its more traditional mortgage suite segment. But growth has lagged behind, posting a YoY decline of 2.8%.

The consumer banking suite is focused primarily on account openings, consumer lending, and account funding. Recently BLND also expanded into offering business deposit accounts after a very strong Q2 with over 23 deals made with new banks/institutions. The business accounts could be particularly lucrative, I think, if we see interest rates fall, which could stimulate investments by smaller companies.

In the mortgage suite, BLND focuses on automating and improving essentially every step of the loan process for real estate. Be that origination, loan processing, or offering support for its lenders.

Target Audience

BLND has a massive partnership catalog with names such as Alloy or Equifax, which offer onboarding automation and credit reporting. It’s these partnerships that have saved BLND's profitability the last few quarters. It saw its add-on products plummet after Q2 2024 but was largely offset by partnerships adding back value. This refers to the mortgage suite, which, as we know, fell YoY in revenues. It's a measure of value per funded loan, which peaked at $99 last year but dipped down to $88.

As for the customers it has, you have Harris Bank, for instance, which mentions a 53% increase in loan origination after starting to use BLND, along with shaving off 5 days in the cycle time.

In the corporate presentation, BLND mentions that banks are spending $5.2 billion annually on originating loans. This presents a very lucrative market for BLND where banks could lower their spending by going through BLND's platforms instead. The exact TAM is unclear still since it touches on so many different types, but it's somewhere between $5 and $9 billion, depending on the region. Based on the last 12 months, BLND captures about 2.38% of this midrange estimate. The biggest competitor is ICE Mortgage Technology, which is a massive loan origination provider. It processes around 30-35% of all US mortgage applications in 2024.

Qualities of Revenues and Growth Sensitivity Analysis

Much of the revenue is SaaS-based, meaning it is highly recurring to a certain degree. If the market keeps ticking on and people still take on loans, much of BLND's revenue will continue to grow. A broader downturn would instead signal a weaker consumer profile, and then we would have other issues in the market instead.

Looking at the first 6 months, 92% of revenues came from its software platforms, the remaining from professional services. Gross margins here sit at 77% YTD, based on $12.3 million cost of sales. YoY declined by 50 bps but was offset by the overall revenue amount growing 11.2% instead.

There has been some real progress in reducing operating expenses. In the first 6 months of 2024, they sat at $70 million but have declined to $54 million thanks to cutbacks in both R&D and sales & marketing. There is still a high headcount at the company, and the management has made it clear they plan on reducing it in order to reduce OPEX further. Right now it needs to cut back at least $10-11 million to get a positive operating income.

During 2024, BLND announced its intention to reduce headcount, which during the year sat at 540. Given the annual SG&A cost of $49 million, it results in the average employee costing $92,000 per year. During 2025, it's cutting back on 5%, which should save it $2.45 million in this department. This is not enough, and we will likely see more cutbacks in marketing and R&D again.

Part of the problem is that BLND simply needs to scale revenues more. Since 2022, the SG&A costs have gone from $224 million to $83 million and revenues from $235 million to $167 million. Software sales, particularly in consumer lending, helped directly improve gross profits. Let me expand. Software revenues grew $5.4 million for the first 6 months, cost of sales for this being $1.3 million.

So by scaling, BLND is essentially adding $4.1 million, or $2.05 million quarterly, in gross profits. The inflection point will be some time next year when gross profits are higher than OPEX. My estimate is in Q3 at the latest if BLND keeps up with cutting back on headcount and other expenses. At this year's momentum, revenue growth/scaling will have naturally grown gross profits by at least $8.2 million. I mentioned earlier that there was a $10-11 million gap between OPEX and gross profits. This will be filled if cost savings and revenue growth keep up. The market seems to have realized the same thing too as the shares rebounded not even a month after the last report.

Conservative WACC Model Shows No Large Upside or Downside

I’ve calculated the WACC as 11.97%, based on $860 million equity value, $2.38 million debt, 12% cost of equity, 0.5% cost of debt, and 1% tax rate. The low debt position, along with low effective tax rates due to certain deferred assets, leads to a WACC model generally being determined by the allowed cost of equity. I've set this at 12% to reflect the necessary return when considering the risks like the historical underperformance to both SPY and XLF.

Using a WACC and growth sensitivity model, the current stock price offers little upside or downside based on my conservative estimate. As per SA analysts revenue forecasts, it will take a hit between 2024 and 2025 but then recover next year, posting 25% YoY top-line growth. This seems to be based much on a recovery in the mortgage origination market. BLND sees a recovery as well but has a more conservative estimate instead of 10% YoY for Q3 2025. Next year it might grow to 12-15%, depending on how well BLND can scale from the recovery.

With a more dovish FED, I can see that argument that the market might recover next year, but I’d be more lenient to go with a revenue forecast closer to the management's expectations.

With riskier investments, I increase the Cost of Equity to reflect this. At the midpoint in the table, 11.97%, paired with a 11.5% CAGR of revenues, I get a fair value of $3.23. This leaves little downside, which means a sell is not yet justified in my opinion. We might see my revenue forecast surpassed, which would mean today's price might be very attractive if WACC stays the same and there are no changes to the debt profile.

If I make a more bullish revenue table, shares show a decent upside. For example, if BLND can scale and reach a 20% CAGR in revenues for 3 years, the price target moves up to $4.07 instead, leaving a 24% upside from today's price levels.

In my opinion, the buy thesis relies almost solely on BLND being able to scale faster if market conditions improve. Given current expectations, the stock seems best rated as a hold with a conservative fair value of $3.23 and a bullish fair value target of $4.07.

Risks

The biggest risk is lack of bank/institution adoption and lower net value per loan for BLND. As I’ve outlined, it really needs to scale revenues to bridge the gap between gross profits and OPEX. Continuing to operate with a negative operating income will deteriorate the company over the long term. The cash and short-term equivalents only cover 4-6 quarters of OPEX right now. Since 2022 it has been bleeding cash at a worrisome rate, all the while revenues ticked down.

Without a profitable bottom line, the company will always be a risky investment. There’s no getting around that. But when that will change is what makes or breaks the investment case here. The market seems to think it will be this year, with BLND's Q3 outlook pinning it at $3-4.5 million non-GAAP operating income. I’ll be cautious and say next year instead costs like sales & marketing might not go down if BLND sees slower adoption. Any disruption in growth will likely spike the short interest along with sending shares further down. The risks weighed against the potential, I think, make it a hold now.

The Full Picture

Shares have been pummeled during 2025, but they have mounted a slight rebound after upbeat guidance and strong consumer lending growth. This is where BLND will see most of its success. A consumer willing to spend more will mean more revenues for BLND. This might come from lower interest rates or more consumer optimism. Right now it needs to focus on making sure it can generate a profit. Until it can do so for at least 4 quarters straight, I won’t be a buyer but instead rate it a hold.

data.title: Something Feels Off - And Billionaires Know It
data.description:
Introduction

During my last months as an international business administration bachelor student, I did a six-month internship. It wasn’t part of the standard curriculum, but it was something I really wanted to do. That’s one of the reasons why finishing my bachelor’s degree took me a bit longer than anticipated. It was worth it, though, as I became a corporate treasury intern at one of Germany’s largest consumer goods companies, which was six months that truly changed my life.

  • Because we were understaffed at the time, I got to do the work of two interns, which gave me insights I never thought I would get and a workload I didn’t think I could handle.
  • It really stirred my interest in supply chains as I saw where the company got its raw materials, how these expenses were hedged, and just how complex Germany’s industrial heart was. I also walked past countless trucks every morning from the parking lot to the office. It was fascinating to see.
  • I first-hand saw what the other side of paying dividends looks like, as I monitored the company’s cash balances every day. It looks a bit like my bank account when I bought my first car, except much bigger.

There are two reasons I’m bringing this up. One is that I thought it wouldn’t hurt to tell you a bit about me and the factors that shaped my career, as I now have more than 45,500 followers, with thousands of new readers in recent months. Thank you for that!

Moreover, on my last day, I had a great chat with my boss, whom I greatly admired (I still do). While I don’t remember the exact quote, he told me to focus on what matters. He explained that we have a total information overload compared to the "olden days" that we need to deal with. I found some evidence of that, as the BBC wrote in 2013 that a person in a major Western city is exposed to as much data in a single day as someone in the 15th century in their entire life (!!!).

That was in 2013, when cloud computing started to heat up. Nowadays, we have AI, which creates an even bigger data overload. Although it’s tough to find supporting data, I found the chart below, which shows that the total amount of data generated has gone from 18 zetabytes (18 trillion gigabytes) to 149 zetabytes in 2024. That’s a 30% annual growth rate.

Between now and 2028, that number is expected to grow by 1.6x.

It’s crazy if you think about how quickly data generation is. AI is now creating life-like videos, writing entire essays within seconds, and mining data at a pace nobody thought possible.

And it’s just the start if you ask me.

Anyway, since I got this advice, I have spent a lot of time erasing "noise," which is why I have a big picture strategy. I don’t care about short-term market movements, near-term interest rate predictions, or anything related to making a quick buck or trying to get everything right. I care about the long-term and finding opportunities that others overlook.

I believe this is now more important than ever as the market has become a circus. We’re dealing with so many trends and developments, most of which are directly related and impacting each other. This includes sticky inflation, horrible consumer sentiment, a stuck housing market, increasingly challenging employment numbers, and a ton of secular growth in anything related to AI.

Oh, and gold has skyrocketed while the market trades at nearly 23.0x earnings.

That’s why important voices are getting louder. This includes Paul Tudor Jones, Ken Griffin, and others who have made some headlines in recent days and weeks.

In this article, I’ll focus on their comments and explain what this could mean for you and me, as I think most people reading this rely on prudent capital allocation to reach their financial goals.

So, let’s get to it!

Party Like It’s 1999

The subtitle I just used is a famous line from the Prince song "1999." However, it also seems to be the market’s anthem, as many are convinced we’re once again dealing with a tech bubble. On Oct. 6, for example, legendary trader Paul Tudor Jones (often referred to as "PTJ") was interviewed by CNBC, who later went with the title "Paul Tudor Jones says ingredients are in place for massive rally before a ‘blow off’ top to bull market."

Essentially, the billionaire makes the case that the foundation is perfect for a strong rally, potentially much more explosive than the one the market witnessed in the year 1999.

There are three things that PTJ noted:

  • Massive returns in tech stocks. It’s hard to disagree with that as the tech-heavy QQQ ETF (QQQ) is up 18% year-to-date and up 25% over the past 12 months. It’s up nearly 510% over the past 10 years. Even the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK) is up nearly 60% year-to-date.
  • Speculative behavior. I disagree with him here. No major indicator shows elevated sentiment, including the CNN Greed & Fear Index, the AAII index, and the University of Michigan consumer confidence report, which also asks people if they expect higher stock prices in the future (see below). However, if we look at massive gains in stocks like AST SpaceMobile (ASTS), Rocket Lab (RKLB), and others, I think it’s still fair to say that sentiment is very bullish.

  • Circular deals are worrisome. While I'm not accusing any company of any wrongdoing (I think PTJ isn’t, either), I see his point, as companies in the AI space are all somewhat connected, which means multi-billion dollar deals all end up benefiting each other.

This is what Morningstar wrote with regard to the latest Nvidia (NVDA) and OpenAI deal, noting in the same article that it does not expect this to be a case of a circular "closed loop" system:

Quite simply, these are deals between two or more parties where funds are flowing back and forth. The most notable recent example is Nvidia’s NVDA partnership with OpenAI, wherein Nvidia can invest up to $100 billion in the firm over time. The partnership can be considered circular, as OpenAI will likely buy gear from Nvidia, which will reinvest those profits in OpenAI, which will likely use those funds to buy even more Nvidia gear. - Morningstar

The overview below has been shared a lot on social media. I believe it perfectly shows how connected the biggest AI players are:

Moreover, there are big differences. While the valuation is comparable to 1999, as the JPMorgan (JPM) chart in this article showed (all else excluded), 1999 saw a U.S. budget surplus. Currently, the U.S. is running crisis-like deficits.

This is one of the reasons why I believe the government will have an interest in keeping inflation higher for longer, it means using lower rates to kickstart cyclical growth, lower the real value of the national debt, and ease the pain of high deficits by stimulating nominal growth. I discussed all of this and more in this recent article, but more on that later.

That’s one of the reasons why PTJ isn’t bearish just yet. While he believes in a blowoff top, he also sees higher prices until that happens, which is why he’s bullish on a combination of gold, cryptocurrencies, and Nasdaq tech stocks between now and the end of the year.

This Isn’t A Normal Environment

PTJ isn’t the only one bullish on gold, which may not come as a surprise, given that the gold chart looks like this:

Ken Griffin, head of Citadel, recently made the case that the trend where people see gold as a safer asset than the dollar is "really concerning." Essentially, he made the case that we are seeing de-dollarization and general de-risking of U.S. sovereign risks. Although I do not believe the dollar will see a weakening reserve status, there’s some merit to these comments:

  • The government shutdown has created elevated uncertainty.
  • Both the deficit and the national debt as a percentage of GDP are at high levels.
  • Rate cuts and dovish talk have created looser financial conditions (see below).

Moreover, as my personal view is that higher inflation will be accepted to get the housing market unstuck, kickstart cyclical/nominal growth, fund the data center buildout, and lower the real value of the national debt, I think gold makes for a great alternative to the dollar.

While it may be a stretch to say that billionaire investor Griffin shares my exact thesis, he made the case that current stimulus (fiscal and monetary) is similar to what happens during a recession, yet we’re clearly not in a recession. That’s why gold is so loved.

It also explains why investors are perfectly fine paying a big premium for stocks.

He also got support from yet another billionaire investor, who most of you may be familiar with, as Ray Dalio made the case that this is an environment similar to the 1970s, when high inflation and economic instability were a huge risk. Back then, stagflation was a real risk. I don’t see that happening, but he’s certainly right when it comes to the toxic mix of inflation and growth risks.

However, Dalio goes even further as he also made the case that the current AI trend gives him feelings of past bubbles, similar to what happened in the late 1920s when patent activity and technological innovation caused speculation to run hot, as well as the Dot-com bubble.

I agree with that, as I'm convinced that a large number of AI stocks that run hot will come down hard once AI gets more mature and we find out who’s swimming naked when the tide goes out, so to speak.

That said, it would be a huge mistake to simply ignore AI because of bubble risks. I have written so many articles on the sheer size and disruptive power of this trend that we all know the economy now will look very different compared to what we will encounter a few years from now. In fact, Walmart (WMT), which already said that every job imaginable can be disrupted, is already working on shipping humanoid robots in the United States.

For now, however, people keep bringing up reports of low adoption rates as an argument that we are indeed in a bubble, as the data so far shows that companies are increasingly throwing hundreds of billions of dollars at a technology with a low return, as The Wall Street Journal wrote:

An MIT report found 95% of organizations surveyed are getting no return on their AI product investments. A University of Chicago economics paper found AI chatbots had “no significant impact on workers’ earnings, recorded hours, or wages” at 7,000 Danish workplaces.

OpenAI’s release of ChatGPT-5 in August was widely viewed as an incremental improvement, not the game-changing moment many expected. Given the high cost of developing it, the release fanned concerns that generative AI models are improving at a slower pace than expected. - WSJ

In fact, many of my readers brought up the surveys from the quote above as well.

This really sounds like a bubble, doesn’t it?

However, I think it isn’t, as we are just in the early stages of AI development. And, in general, it always takes time for large corporations to make changes. Not only do big changes have to go through many committees, but we are also at a point where AI is not able to actually replace labor or think on its own. That’s something we’ll likely see starting next year.

Even last year, people like the CEO of Anthropic made the case that intelligent AI won’t be widespread until 2026, when we will see a rapid movement toward AI that’s smarter than humans.

So, to go back to Dalio, despite his bubble call, he seems to agree with a thesis I have laid out a few times this year, which is that we need to focus on two kinds of companies:

  • Companies that are able to produce large-scale efficiencies. Think of companies like Amazon (AMZN), Walmart, Union Pacific (UNP), PepsiCo (PEP), JPMorgan (JPM), and others that have huge and mature businesses with a moat. They can leverage existing capabilities and cut costs in logistics, payroll, warehousing, etc.
  • Providers of AI technology. Here, I would put Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Microsoft (MSFT), among many others. Companies that have proven AI models that are being widely adopted. They can be scaled rapidly, as Google’s Gemini has shown.

Readers who know me expect a third bullet point here. They're correct.

  • If there’s anything I’m bullish on beyond companies that adopt AI in an efficient way, it’s the players that solve the bottlenecks. This includes power producers, energy companies, infrastructure giants, cooling technology suppliers, and others. To throw some names at you, I’m thinking of Comfort Systems USA (FIX), which I own, EMCOR Group (EME), Quanta Services (PWR), Vertiv Holdings (VRT), Range Resources (RRC), CoreWeave (CRWV), Tesla (TSLA), and (countless) others.

Keep these three bullet points in mind as I bring up yet another billionaire, Amazon founder Jeff Bezos. He also said we’re in a bubble, while noting that when the dust settles, we will see who actually has the tools that benefit society, which is one of the benefits of the private market. While bubbles may occur, they still lead to efficient capital allocation over time.

Jeff Bezos made headlines on Friday when he heralded an AI “industrial bubble.” There’s no question valuations are stretched, reminiscent of the dot-com days. Who knows if OpenAI and other developers will be able to generate the revenue to pay for their massive investments. And some companies might fail.

But as the Amazon founder noted, “when the dust settles and you see who are the winners, society benefits from those inventions.” It’s far better that private markets drive investment than government, as was the case with the electric-vehicle bubble created during the Biden years. - WSJ

Keep It Simple to Generate Alpha

To get back to the core of this article, I think most billionaire investors would agree with my articles this year, as they have all become careful with market valuations, yet none of them refrain from buying stocks.

There’s simply too much value, and I also believe we have to make money on this AI trend, as the disruption that could follow might have a profound impact on the market and economy.

However, we don’t have to overthink it. As Bezos made clear, once the dust settles, we’ll see what companies have made the right choices when it comes to AI capex and implementation. That’s why I think the job of investors who like to pick their own stocks instead of buying index funds should consist of these things:

  • Build a strong core of companies that are disruption-proof. I have done this by investing in wide-moat companies in critical supply chains, including Class I railroads, industrial supply, defense contractors, and energy companies, among others.
  • Figure out what companies will still be winners once the dust settles. I try to accomplish this by investing in companies with minimal competition. For example, instead of trying to figure out who will win the chip race, I invest in Comfort Systems USA, which supports data center construction. As long as data center demand is high, I don’t care who’s the best producer of chips. Don’t overthink it and play the long game. That’s truly my biggest takeaway for this specific group of stocks.
  • Be aware of why the market is trading the way it’s trading. Essentially, I’m saying that both gold and stocks continue to see high demand, as investors know inflation hasn’t been beaten. If anything, we should brace ourselves for “higher for longer” inflation. While I consider gold to be somewhat overbought on the short term, I think hard assets with inflation protection remain the go-to place for the coming years, including silver, miners, streamers like Franco-Nevada (FNV), energy companies like Diamondback Energy (FANG), which I expect to be a takeover target, and even real estate with pricing power like Rexford Industrial (REXR).

I think the biggest mistake investors can make is being convinced the market is in a bubble, which means moving to cash. I also believe risks lie in trying too hard to find the next Nvidia (you know what I mean). Focus on what works, identify bottlenecks, and keep a well-balanced portfolio that fits your investment strategy.

Meanwhile, I’ll try to keep making sense of this market and economic environment in my daily articles and provide you with more food for thought.

Risks to My Thesis

The risks are straightforward, as they mainly consist of recession and AI adoption risks. An unexpected recession could put pressure on the market, in general, and hurt cyclical stocks, including energy and transportation.

data.title: Carvana: Not Time To Take The Foot Off The Gas
data.description:
Car retailer giant Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) is at a point of very solid execution, following the consolidation of its 2023 and 2024 recovery, with record GPU (~7,580/unit) and highly efficient inventory with turnover at its all-time high. The macro environment, with possible interest rate cuts ahead and used car prices still high are tailwind in terms of demand and margins for maintaining solid execution.

Despite trading at fairly high traditional valuation multiples, the Tempe, Arizona-based company's projected top-line growth (of 24% CAGR according to consensus for the next five years), when added to its operating leverage, tends to still reflect a significant upside. Especially if we consider the scenario of continued improvements in GPUs, which automatically implies a more robust EPS—and likely once again surpassing analysts' projections.

Finally, CVNA finds itself in a technical and psychological momentum, which, combined with consistent execution, suggests that the company arrives very well positioned to capture additional earnings in Q3 (with earnings release scheduled for the end of October) while maintaining sustainable growth in the medium term, at least, even with the ongoing volatility that accompanies Carvana stock as usual in recent years.

A Rapid Timeline on Carvana

We can divide Carvana's thesis into three key periods that show just how wild the company's performance has been since it became a publicly traded company in 2017.

Between 2018 and 2021, Carvana went through an explosive growth phase, pursuing hyper-scaling and containing negative cash flows, as it presented to the market its business model based on used-car retailing in a vertically integrated manner into inventory, logistics, and financing. As a result, there was a massive expansion of inspection centers and delivery infrastructure nationwide, mostly funded by a combination of debt and share issues, as well as limited reinvestment of operating cash.

Carvana strategically managed to grow vehicle sales (top line) very quickly. But at the cost of hurting its bottom line, given the very negative levels of EBIT and free cash flow. It's important to note, however, that at the time, the macroeconomic scenario in the U.S. was very favorable to used car sales: lower rates, cheap financing, and also a booming market, especially during the pandemic years.

The second stage was one of chaos, when Carvana saw death up close.

The year 2022 came with a liquidity crisis and a total collapse of the bull case. There was a macroeconomic shock as rates jumped sharply to contain post-pandemic inflation, used-car prices began to normalize, and demand began to cool.

Thus, Carvana was left with a very expensive inventory bought at peak prices-something that almost decimated the company. EBITDA figures became extremely negative, and cash burn soared to unprecedented levels as the credit market tightened. Free cash flow went negative by as much as $1.8 billion (down 43% versus 2021), and the company's cash was down to $316 million—a reduction of $734 million from the previous year.

The result was a stock collapse of more than 95% from its highs, with bankruptcy fears becoming increasingly real in that period. As a result, Carvana was forced to slow down its growth and expand liquidity. To deal with the crisis, the initiative was to issue $3.3 billion in senior unsecured notes with an interest rate of 10.25% in April 2022, in addition to a series of cost containment measures, including mass layoffs.

The third stage, from 2023 to 2024, could be labeled as the Phoenix rising from the ashes.

In addition to relying on a sharply rebounded macroeconomic scenario, the management team implemented a strategic shift, with a much more cautious expansion and a priority focus on cost efficiency and Gross Profit per Unit (GPU) maximization. Little by little, Carvana has climbed to a GPU above $5,000 per unit, and the continuation of these levels throughout 2023 to the present. And no miracle was needed for that.

As the graph above shows, the feat of returning GPU so sharply up at the end of 2023 had retail sales as the main profit engine, as a reflection of the management team focusing on selling more cars directly to consumers rather than pushing inventory to auctions. In addition, the advent of upsell add-ons and other financing products also helped increase revenue per unit. And the combination of retail plus the "other" segment, which includes (1) financing margins; (2) extended warranties; and (3) ancillary services such as car delivery, increased total GPU, especially as volumes stabilized.

Wholesale (selling to auctions or third parties), which was the smallest contributor to Carvana's rebound-and which was used during the 2022 crisis to offload excess inventory-was intentionally reduced in terms of reliance to protect GPU, something I attribute as a clever and correct strategy.

Due to the fact that Carvana was, especially during this period, a much-followed case by retail traders and hedge funds, being taken over by an emotional "boom-bust" narrative, the price swings amounted to a jaw-dropping 10,000% from lows to highs.

The Bull Case for Carvana Isn’t Done Yet

At the current juncture, I believe that when approaching Carvana, the following points need to be weighed up: (1) the current macroeconomic context in the automotive market; (2) developments in Gross Profit per Vehicle (GPU); (3) the efficiency of the current inventory.

When we look at interest rates and inflation, the Fed signaling a possible 50 bps reduction in interest rates in the fourth quarter of 2025, with the aim of stimulating consumption and relieving pressure on consumers, is a very welcome scenario, and arguably one of the main drivers of CVNA shares over the past few months.

In the used car market, volatility in the used car market has been exacerbated by tariff policies, but the average price in October, at $25,512 in the U.S., reflects a 25-week consecutive decline, attributed to a slowdown in demand and market adjustments.

Notably, the Manheim Used Vehicle Value Index (MUVVI) fell to 207 in September this year, implying a decrease of 0.2% compared to August, but still 2% above the previous year's level. For Carvana, the slight drop in the index could put pressure on margins if sales prices are not adjusted or inventory is not controlled. In other words, arguably the most reasonable strategy still tends to focus on retail + other services (financing, warranties, delivery), which contribute more to profit than the simple resale price.

Regarding the GPU, the image below shows that the retail GPU has practically doubled in the last two years. But even more impressively, total non-GAAP GPU reached an all-time high in Q2 2025, with $7,580 per unit sold. As we can also see, Carvana has reduced reconditioning and SG&A costs per unit, being a major driver of margin. And even though the used-vehicle market may be fluctuating (and potentially weak), Carvana still sold 143,280 units in Q2 2025—a growth of 41% year-on-year.

In my view, based on Carvana's own projections (sales growing slightly sequentially) and market data, I wouldn't be surprised to see retail GPU remain above $3,000 in Q3 and Total GPU non-GAAP approach or even exceed $7,600.

From an out-year perspective, I still find the level of repricing that the market continues to apply to Carvana quite bizarre, even after what happened between 2022 and 2024.

See in the table below how EPS estimates have been revised upward in the last six months by percentages of 65%, 51%, and 49%, respectively, for the current year up to 2027.

One possible explanation for these bullish revisions is linked to the learning curve and the operational leverage of the thesis, where each increase in the GPU has a leveraged impact on the EPS, because a large part of the fixed costs is already covered. Thus, small operational improvements overall significantly reschedule profit, justifying the robust revisions.

Shifting gears into inventory, as Carvana needs to buy vehicles at market prices and sell them quickly so as not to tie up cash, in the last twelve months, the company has an inventory turnover of 10.62x, at its all-time highs, which implies a Days Inventory Outstanding (DIO) of 34.4 days—or each car staying in stock for a month and a week.

This tends to be an aggressive turnover, higher than the average used car retailer, with a turnover of between 5x and 8x. The caveat here is that a very high turnover signals pressure to buy cars quickly, possibly paying more. So, balance with GPU (keeping above $7,000) and refueling capacity is key.

Valuations Show Stretch, but Growth Justifies the Price

That said, when looking at Carvana's multiples, it's shocking how the company seems overvalued by nearly every single traditional valuation metric. Even if the market estimates for 2028 with Carvana potentially delivering EPS of 10.38 (double the 2025 expectation), the stock would still trade at a multiple of 35x, something much higher than the average for the automotive retail sector, which currently ranges at 17.6x.

However, based on the top-line consensus for Carvana for the next five years, we can speculate on an outcome that is not necessarily out of the ordinary.

For example, analysts estimate revenues growing at a CAGR of 24.4% over the next five years.

Putting these projections into a 5-year reverse DCF model, I will also anticipate (somewhat plausibly) that EBIT margins could expand from the current 9.2% (ttm) by 100 bps from year one to year three (2025 to 2028) and then remain flat. Given a 16% tax rate (due to expected tax planning and NOL utilization), conservatively assuming no changes in working capital, a WACC of 7.4%, and perpetuity growth of 3%, at a minimum, this would justify an equity value of $80.9 billion—about ~4.2% higher than what the company is worth today (at last check).

As we can see below, achieving and maintaining 12% operating margins would be something unprecedented in the retail auto industry, but I would say that Carvana is quite close, and the trend is undoubtedly upwards-including with the management team guiding a 13.5% EBITDA margin within five to ten years.

I'd say that if you keep up the pace of operational expansion and cost control, reaching 12% EBIT margins by 2026 or 2027 is totally feasible, and you can even put some of the assumptions adopted in the DCF as conservative.

Risks and Final Remarks

Carvana is a highly volatile stock by its nature, around 3.5x more volatile than the broader market (5-year monthly beta) over the last five years, which tends to make predictability difficult and can put off more conservative institutional investors. The fact that its bold targets depend on flawless execution in logistics and reconditioning, any bottlenecks tend to go directly towards margins—especially in an environment of accelerated growth.

But as the company's Q3 earnings approach, scheduled for October 29, the odds remain mostly bullish. A quick look at the moving averages shows Carvana in a confirmed long-term uptrend, above the 200-day, as well as the 50-day moving average, also above the 200-day, showing another sign of structural strength. Having reached its all-time high (~$395 per share) as recently as October 1, I believe that a psychological barrier has formed that has reduced short-term demand, generating a temporary drop, highlighted by the 20-day moving averages.

Acknowledging that short-term volatility should be routine in Carvana's thesis, I believe that in the long term, this is an extremely well-placed company to continue growing both top line and bottom line. The macroeconomic momentum is slightly favorable, and the execution seems to be going in the right direction to achieve long-term goals. Valuations may be stretched at first, but given the options in the thesis and based on a consensus that may even be leaning towards conservatism, I believe there is still room for a significant upside ahead.

data.title: UiPath's Historic AI Pivot Won Me Over (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

It’s been a while since I wrote about the automation platform company, UiPath (NYSE:PATH).

In May last year, I explained why I expected “the company’s revenue run rates to mature over time" as its large enterprise customers would slow spending on UiPath’s platform. Since my previous coverage, UiPath’s shares fell, and without the lack of meaningful catalysts over the last 2-3 quarters, I did not feel compelled to update my thesis on the company.

But I have been following the company, especially the recent round of updates the company has shared, and I believe the company is set to see its first signs of meaningful inflection in its ARR by the end of CY25. The company has realized the value of industry partnerships in the age of AI and has been moving quickly.

Per my observations, markets are discounting the coming inflection in its ARR, giving investors an opportunistic entry point in UiPath’s shares.

I am upgrading UiPath to a Buy.

What Is UiPath Doing Differently This Time?

Investors are quite familiar with cloud services such as companies such as ServiceNow (NOW) or Snowflake (SNOW) that have done reasonably well for themselves in the age of AI versus most of the other players in the cloud services industry.

In my view, one of the reasons these cloud services companies have succeeded when others have not is because they moved quickly to forge partnerships with important industry leaders in the same market or adjacent markets to create micro AI ecosystems. The big benefit of these industry partnerships in the age of AI is that it enhances mutual productivity and accelerate AI-powered innovations at scale, eventually becoming accretive to the revenue bases of all partners within the micro ecosystem, as MIT Sloan suggests. Companies like ServiceNow or Snowflake moved quickly to partner with other rapidly growing AI beneficiaries such as Nvidia (NVDA) or Microsoft (MSFT) early on.

So far, UiPath has never really understood the value strategic partnerships could provide, in my view, and has relied on a legacy-based revenue-sourcing framework of completely leaning on GSIs (global system integrators). GSIs are business partners, typically value-added resellers and/or business consultants like Deloitte, Accenture (ACN), etc.

When asked about the impact these partners were having on UiPath’s sales at a conference early last year, here is how management reasoned for the case of partnering with GSIs:

GSIs, they are a great introductory into customers where they are in the midst of big transformation process projects of which automation can be an important part of that.

Automation can play a key role in driving success and accelerating that migration. So, we're super excited. It's early stages, gone through enablement, education, there is a quota component, so they can retire quotas, as part of co-selling automation.

In the same conference they even announced the hiring of Bronwyn Hastings, “a seasoned executive who has built very large channel and partner models.”

21 months later, Ms. Hastings appears to have moved out of UiPath, while the company’s ARR kept decelerating every single quarter, strongly indicating how the GSI sales strategy failed UiPath in lifting up their revenues & ARR.

However, all of that has changed, based on the recent slate of updates that I noticed in UiPath’s IR page. Here are all the announcements UiPath made on a single day in September:

  • Allowing customers to automate end-to-end processes using UiPath agents interacting with Microsoft’s Azure AI Foundry agents and models.

  • Uniting UiPath’s Agentic Automation platform with Snowflake’s Cortex AI platform of AI agents

  • Expanding UiPath’s Agentic Automation capabilities into Alphabet-owned (GOOG) Google Gemini.

  • And the big one is collaborating with Nvidia (NVDA) to bring automation capabilities to Nvidia’s automation platform Nemotron as well as to Nvidia’s NIM, the microservices inference platform.

All these partnerships indicate a pivotal shift in UiPath’s sales strategy in forming alliances with some of the largest beneficiaries of the AI ecosystem today, which I strongly believe will give the company’s ARR inflection a much-needed facelift. I cannot recall a time in the company’s history where UiPath has made such strong shifts away from its usual GSI-led revenue engine.

I suspect management is already accounting for a small boost on the backdrop of these partnerships, which is why they have guided ARR to inflect higher and be positive for its first acceleration of many years in Q4 this year. UiPath is banking on its agentic automation product Maestro, which automates agentic orchestration and the process of building AI agents.

Here is another way. I have charted the y/y growth in ARR and TTM revenues, which factors in management’s FY26 revenue guidance of $1.6B and FY26 ARR outlook of $1.8B.

If ARR inflects towards the trajectory that management has forecasted, I would not be surprised if UiPath’s FY26 revenues eventually come in higher than the $1.6B revenue guidance.

Valuations Indicate UiPath’s Multiple Could Expand

At the moment, UiPath’s shares trade at a forward-looking revenue multiple of 4.9x FY26 revenues of $1.6B, growing 10% y/y. This is slightly under the 24-month average forward revenue multiple of 5.1-5.8x forward revenues.

Markets have also become optimistic about UiPath’s strategic shift away from GSI partners towards industry alliances with leading AI beneficiaries, which explains the ~30% expansion in revenue multiples in the past 30 days.

But if investors observe the forward-looking revenue multiple for FY27, they will observe that UiPath’s revenue multiple of ~4.5x FY27 revenues worth $1.7B, growing 8.5%, is still cheap. With ARR moving up, I suspect analysts will eventually upgrade their revenue outlook for next year, possibly expecting ~10% y/y growth in FY27 as well. That implies a forward valuation of 5.1-5.5x FY27 revenues, above the 4.5x UiPath’s shares currently trade at. This suggests a PT of $19.6 per share, or ~15% upside from current levels of ~$17.

Risks & Other Factors To Note

As optimistic as I’d like to be, I have to admit, I’m not fond of the broad-based insider selling that can be seen in UiPath’s shares. It does not echo conviction shared by management in their own shares at a time when I do see signs of an inflection in the business. It doesn’t stop my analysis from recommending a Buy rating but it is a risk investors should note nevertheless.

Takeaway

I am beginning to like UiPath’s strategy to position itself as a beneficiary of the AI revolution. I think they have one of the best shots at regaining control of their growth narrative in the age of AI to offer their agentic automation products to customers.

I am recommending a Buy rating on UiPath.

data.title: MARA Holdings: The Bitcoin Miner Hiding An AI Empire
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Since my previous coverage, Marathon (NASDAQ:MARA) is up by 32%, but it only trades at ~9x EV/EBITDA, well below peers. The market only sees it as a pure Bitcoin (BTC-USD) miner, failing to recognize the transition into an AI-prepared energy infrastructure firm.

With owned capacity of 1.1 GW, $0.04/kWh tied-up energy costs, and partnerships with TAE Power Solutions as well as EDF’s Exaion, MARA is developing variable, low-cost compute capacity that is able to serve AI workloads. Once the said assets come online to have recurring AI revenues, MARA’s multiple is able to re-rate fast, catching up with the likes of IREN (IREN) and propelling the next leg of the supercycle.

Why MARA’s AI Bull Case Remains Hidden, But May Explode Soon

Consensus argues the data-center gold rush has only taken any miner with megawatts and a press release. The stock market has only been rewarding the companies that have already demonstrated an ability to convert power interconnects into contracted, GPU-monetized cash flows, before the next capacity constraint, not those with big Bitcoin treasuries or theoretical “AI optionality.” That’s why MARA's meteoric operating performance hasn't translated into a commensurate multiple while the IREN has re-rated sharply.

IREN approved a dual-engine architecture, Bitcoin mining supplemented by an AI cloud business with labeled GPUs, liquid-cooling roadmaps, and fresh multi-year contracts, behind 810 MW of currently operating data-center capacity and 50 EH/s by mid-2025. Its SEC filing also records thousands of H100/H200 operational and Blackwell-class GPUs on order, with short-term commissioning targets. That hardware-plus-contract proof, multiplied by an out-year convert zero-coupon, out-priced amid strong demand, has created a reflexive loop within IREN’s equity.

MARA, meanwhile, has produced record profitability and a balance sheet fortress, $238.5 million of Q2 revenue (+64% YoY), $1.2 billion adjusted EBITDA, $808 million net income, 57.4 EH/s, and 49,951 BTC treasury, but the quarter's profit was overshadowed by an unexpected $1.2 billion non-cash mark-to-market gain on digital assets. Equity markets have relegated MARA to the “levered Bitcoin beta” category while they await P&L visibility on AI/HPC dollars, rather than strategy slides.

Management is taking the right steps. It raised capital through 0% convertible notes due 2032, invested in behind-the-meter wind power, and is building partnerships focused on grid management and AI edge computing.

Further, MARA agreed to buy a 64% stake in Exaion, an EDF subsidiary, the French energy giant, in August 2025 for $168 million, with the option to take ownership to 75% by 2027. Exaion runs HPC and artificial intelligence cloud data centers under partnership with Nvidia (NVDA), Deloitte, and 2CRSI, with business from enterprises and governments.

This deal is a transformational step for MARA, it moves the company from pure Bitcoin mining into AI and cloud infrastructure, giving it access to Tier-4, GDPR-compliant data centers and European enterprise clients. In short, it turns MARA’s cheap power and grid flexibility into monetized, AI-ready capacity.

However, monetization is still early. If MARA completes the EDF/Exaion deal and turns its flexible energy capacity into real AI or enterprise contracts, its valuation could rise quickly. Until then, the stock remains closely tied to Bitcoin prices and mining difficulty.

Is MARA’s Formula Really Similar To That Of IREN’s?

They appear the same on the surface with low-cost power, humongous data centers, and AI optionality. Their business approaches, however, are very different. IREN is GPU-centric. It’s already deploying thousands of Nvidia chips and has sealed multi-year AI cloud deals. With liquid-coupled expansion and ERCOT interconnects on the horizon, investors take IREN as a miner cashing in from the AI already. That’s why the recently raised 0% convertible was so well tolerated, the market believes in its AI business but not speculatively.

MARA is grid-focused. It’s deploying mining revenues to construct versatile, inexpensive power infrastructure capable of powering AI data centers. Running 1.1 GW of operational capacity at only $0.04/kWh, MARA is making its power headway into a future moat. The cooperation with TAE Power Solutions is attempting to make the flexible energy load offered by Bitcoin viable for AI demand, while the upcoming Exaion deal has the potential to provide MARA direct access to European sovereign and enterprise customers focused on AI.

In short, both companies raised 0% converts, but MARA’s capability to generate yield from its Bitcoin stack allows it to finance growth without fresh dilution. Thus, so long as investors continue to pursue visible GPU revenue, IREN remains the simpler story. But if the market pivots toward energy versatility and sovereign AI infrastructure, MARA’s thesis is poised to re-rate quickly.

Why MARA Still Trades Below Its True Power Value

At approximately $20 per share, MARA is valued at about 11.6x trailing earnings, 63% off the industry median of 31.2x, and 9.0x EV/EBITDA compared to 19.4x for the group. Cheap by these trailing figures, much backed by all-time-high profitability and benign energy prices. However, forward estimates paint a murkier picture. Analysts expect $0.48 EPS for FY2025, implying a forward P/E near 42x, and revenue of just under $1 billion, translating to a forward P/S of around 7x, about 2x higher than the sector average.

This valuation discrepancy is driven by skepticism over the sustainability of current earnings on an eventual normalizing of non-cash Bitcoin revaluations and shutting post-halving mining margins. The Street obviously doesn't count on one-time marks-to-market benefits that fueled the Q2 $808 million net income. Nevertheless, on an EV/EBITDA basis, however, MARA continues to screen nicely, pricing below half the peer multiple, hinting the underlying business still appears undervalued compared with the sector’s growth names such as IREN or CleanSpark (CLSK).

Between the Halving and the Hype: MARA’s Tightrope Risk

MARA's greatest risk remains that of economic dependence on Bitcoin. Revenues still directly result from the mining of over 90% of the Bitcoin supply, so the profits are extremely dependent on the price of Bitcoin and the difficulty level of the network.

Any sustained decline in the BTC or increase in the global hash rate will quickly constrict margins despite the MARA's low $0.04/kWh power cost. The mining reward decrease coming up in April 2024 has already cut block rewards by half, so the future viability is dependent on the growth of the fee from transactions or other efficiency gain.

Another critical risk is execution on its transition to AI and HPC. The TAE Power Solutions joint venture and planned acquisition of Exaion are strategic but have not yet generated visible revenue. If integration is stalled or if the market for AI compute is anemic, the market may continue to only value MARA as a Bitcoin miner, capturing multiple expansion.

Takeaway

Marathon’s narrative is no longer solely bitcoin’s. MARA is still only ~9x EV/EBITDA, well behind peers, as the market only sees miners. However, its 1.1 GW inexpensive power, Exaion agreement, and AI partnerships put it in an ideal place to transition into enterprise-caliber compute, realizing an impressive re-rating in the next 12 months.

It was a tough five days for Wall Street, as U.S. markets on Friday slumped to their worst week since late May. The decline was primarily driven by a sharp sell-off on Friday, triggered by the resurgence of trade war concerns.
